coredata.dc:title: Engineering red-emissive carbon dots for monitoring lysosomal polarity in living cells and zebrafish
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jianhua
Xing, Jinling
Xu, Hongmei
Yang, Zhenhua
Lu, Wenjing
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Lysosomal polarity is an essential microenvironment parameter in biological systems that is intimately associated to a wide range of cellular statuses. Monitoring polarity fluctuations in lysosomes is extremely meaningful for investigating lysosome-related diseases. Herein, we reported the de novo design and one-step synthesis of lysosome-targeted, red-emissive carbon dots (Lyso-CDs) using Nile Blue A, 3-nitrophenylboronic acid, and 4-morpholinoaniline as precursors, thereby endowing the CDs with abundant electron-donating groups and electron-withdrawing groups on the surface. Specifically, Lyso-CDs showed a high sensitivity to polarity owing to the formation of effective intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) systems, with fluorescence intensity exhibiting a linear relationship against polarity (R2 = 0.9934). By virtue of the advantages, including rapid staining, outstanding biocompatibility, and strong lysosome binding ability, the Lyso-CDs have been applied in imaging the lysosomal polarity changes in cells and zebrafish induced by drugs. Moreover, lysosomal polarity was found to be lower in cancer cells than in healthy cells. Additionally, the Lyso-CDs were effectively applied to track the changes of lysosomal polarity during the autophagy process. More importantly, a gradually increasing trend in lysosomal polarity was demonstrated at zebrafish embryonic development. This work will provide a significant strategy for the design of fluorescent carbon dots in terms of structure and functionality, consequently further promoting the study on lysosome-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Lysosomal polarity
Cell imaging
Zebrafish imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design, characterization, and morphology of the Lyso-CDs
Fluorescence response of the Lyso-CDs to polarity
Ultrarapid lysosome imaging of the Lyso-CDs
Performance of the Lyso-CDs in detecting lysosomal polarity disorders
Monitoring lysosomal polarity during autophagy progress
Fluorescence imaging of polarity in zebrafish

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090 ), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2022Y024 ). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the NMR and Raman analysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.22274090), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2022Y024). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the NMR and Raman analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM image of Lyso-CDs and the particle size distribution histogram. (b) FT-IR spectra of Lyso-CDs. (c) XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS patterns of (d) N 1s, and (e) O 1s of Lyso-CDs. (f) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of Lyso-CDs.

(a) Normalized emission spectra of Lyso-CDs in different solvents. (b) Emission spectra of Lyso-CDs in different 1,4-dioxane/H2O ratios (0–100 %). (c) Linearity of the fluorescence intensity of Lyso-CDs against Δƒ. (d) The fluorescence intensities of Lyso-CDs with various analytes (1.5 mM): (1) Mg2+, (2) K+, (3) Ca2+, (4) Na+, (5) Zn2+, (6) SO3 2−, (7) ClO−, (8) H2O2, (9) NO2 −, (10) S2−, (11) GSH, (12) Cys, (13) Trp, (14) Glu, (15) AA, (16) Asn, (17) Pro, (18) Phe, (19) Ala, (20) Gly, (21) Gln, (22) L-His. (e) The fluorescence intensities of Lyso-CDs under different pH values. (f) The fluorescence intensity in a solution containing water and glycerol.

CLMS images of HeLa cells co-labeled with 10 μg/mL Lyso-CDs and commercial dyes, (a–e) Lyso-Tracker Green (0.3 μM), (f–j) Mito-Tracker Green (0.3 μM). Scale bar: 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a–d) Fluorescence images of HeLa cells treated with Lyso-CDs and stimulated with DTT (80 mM), DMSO (10 μL), and LPS (100 μg/mL). (e) Mean fluorescence intensity obtained from (a1−d1). Scale bar: 20 μm.
(a–i) Confocal microscopic images of cancer cells (HeLa and MCF-7) and normal cells (HL-7702) treated with Lyso-CDs. (j) Mean fluorescence intensities of images a, d, and g. Scale bar: 20 μm.
(a–f) Fluorescence images of Lyso-CDs-stained HeLa cells cultured with rapamycin for 0–90 min, (g) Changing tendency of the mean fluorescence intensity of the images (a–f). Scale bar: 20 μm.

(a–d) Fluorescence images of development stages from live zebrafish embryos and larvae cultured with Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL). (e) Average fluorescence intensity of the images from (a1-d1). Scale bar: 200 μm.

(a–d) Fluorescence images of zebrafish larva labled with Lyso-CDs and Lyso-CDs co-cultured with DTT (10 mM), DMSO (10 μL), and LPS (100 μg/mL). (e) Mean fluorescence intensity of the red channel. Scale bar: 200 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Graphical illustration of the design strategy and the application of the Lyso-CDs in monitoring polarity changes.

head.ce:title: Engineering red-emissive carbon dots for monitoring lysosomal polarity in living cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lysosomal polarity is an essential microenvironment parameter in biological systems that is intimately associated to a wide range of cellular statuses. Monitoring polarity fluctuations in lysosomes is extremely meaningful for investigating lysosome-related diseases. Herein, we reported the de novo design and one-step synthesis of lysosome-targeted, red-emissive carbon dots (Lyso-CDs) using Nile Blue A, 3-nitrophenylboronic acid, and 4-morpholinoaniline as precursors, thereby endowing the CDs with abundant electron-donating groups and electron-withdrawing groups on the surface. Specifically, Lyso-CDs showed a high sensitivity to polarity owing to the formation of effective intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) systems, with fluorescence intensity exhibiting a linear relationship against polarity (R2 = 0.9934). By virtue of the advantages, including rapid staining, outstanding biocompatibility, and strong lysosome binding ability, the Lyso-CDs have been applied in imaging the lysosomal polarity changes in cells and zebrafish induced by drugs. Moreover, lysosomal polarity was found to be lower in cancer cells than in healthy cells. Additionally, the Lyso-CDs were effectively applied to track the changes of lysosomal polarity during the autophagy process. More importantly, a gradually increasing trend in lysosomal polarity was demonstrated at zebrafish embryonic development. This work will provide a significant strategy for the design of fluorescent carbon dots in terms of structure and functionality, consequently further promoting the study on lysosome-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polarity, a crucial microenvironment parameter, can activate functional proteins, trigger signal transduction and membrane systems, thus affecting numerous biological processes in living cells [1–3]. Moreover, abnormal variations in polarity are closely linked to numerous human diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, inflammation, and cancer [4]. Hence, a precise response to polar variations in living organisms would be extremely helpful and significant. Among a range of organs and processes, detecting lysosomal polarity has drawn tremendous attention from researchers, as lysosomes play critical roles in preserving cell homeostasis and shielding cells from stress, starvation, and infection [5,6]. As the functions of lysosomes depend critically on their balanced microenvironment, tracking lysosomal polarity changes is of great significance. It not only affects lysosome in membrane permeability [7], but also determines the biological activities of various molecules [8]. Moreover, studies have revealed that cancer cells have lower polarity than healthy cells [9,10]. Thus, it is extremely desirable to develop a new technology to monitor lysosomal polarity in live cells with the purpose of shedding light on the relationship between lysosome-related physiological and pathological processes.

Fluorescent imaging technology provides an effective method for studying cell dynamics owing to its advantages of high sensitivity, real-time tracking, and simplicity of operation [11–14]. Currently, numerous fluorescent probes were extensively used to image lysosomal polarity [8,15–18]. While most probes possessing lysosomal polarity responses were based on small organic molecules, which have the inherent limitations of complicated organic synthesis, poor water solubility, and photobleaching [19,20], thus blocking them from practical applications of efficient biological tracking and imaging. Overall, it is essential and significant to develop a novel type of lysosomal probe that can achieve imaging polarity in living cells.

Carbon dots (CDs) are considered ideal candidates to monitor lysosomal polarity in consideration of their tunable photoluminescence properties, remarkable photo-stability, simple synthesis procedure, and specific organelle targeting capability [21–24]. A few fluorescent CDs with polarity-sensitive properties have been developed recently [25–27]. For example, Li et al. [25] prepared lysosome-targeted CDs to reveal the lower polarity in cancer cells compared with healthy cells. In addition, Chen et al. [26] synthesized green-emitting CDs possessing a sensitive polarity response function for visualization of polarity variations in cells. These studies illustrate the powerful utility of polarity-sensitive CDs and provide important contributions to biological research. Nevertheless, half of the abovementioned CDs showed blue to green fluorescence with shallow tissue penetration and high interference in autofluorescence [28]. As such, some efforts have been taken to address the challenge by developing red fluorescent CDs [29–32]. Red-emissive CDs can result in lower background signal, deep tissue penetration, reduced light scattering, efficient excitation in biological samples, and negligible irradiation damage to the cells and tissues. Hence, CDs with red emission are more preferable for cell labeling. However, such red-emissive CDs with lysosome apparatus-specific polarity-responsive characteristics are still rarely reported as well as their in vivo applications, appealing for further development in this field [33].

Herein, we designed and synthesized lysosome-targeted CDs with red emission (Lyso-CDs) for monitoring the polarity fluctuation. Specifically, Lyso-CDs were prepared through a one-step solvothermal method using Nile Blue A, 3-nitrophenylboronic acid, and 4-morpholinoaniline as the main precursors by providing CDs with electron-donating groups (e.g., amino and morpholine groups) and electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., boronic and nitro groups) residues on the surface. Due to their efficient ICT effect, Lyso-CDs exhibited a sensitive response to the micro-environment polarity. It could rapidly and selectively bind to lysosomes with a bright red fluorescence. Moreover, Lyso-CDs were successfully employed to visualize the lysosomal polarity changes in cells and zebrafish induced by drugs. More importantly, the cancer cells exhibiting a lower polarity of the lysosome than the healthy cells have been confirmed. Meanwhile, by utilizing Lyso-CDs, we observed the increased polarity during autophagy; in addition, a progressive increase in polarity levels was identified in the zebrafish development process. Taken together, this work is of novelty both from a fundamental and practical research perspective. On one hand, our research proposes a new strategy for the design of CDs with long-wavelength emission, organelle-targeting, and polarity-sensitive property via an elaborate selection of functional precursors, which provides some useful guidance for the facile synthesis of CDs with long-wavelength emission and specific functionalities. On the other hand, this is a meaningful work of by utilizing CDs as lysosome labeling agents for quantification of polarity in different cell status and development stages of zebrafish, which provides a powerful toolbox for investigating diagnosis and treatment strategies for lysosome-related diseases.

Nile blue (10 mg), 3-nitrophenylboronic acid (100 mg), and 4-morpholinoaniline (10 mg) were dispersed ultrasonically in 20 mL of ethanol. Then, this resulting mixture was poured into an autoclave equipped with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling, the color of the cherry mixture was obtained, which was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 15 min) and then filtered (0.22 μm micron filter) to discard large aggregations. After that, the supernatant was collected in a dialysis membrane (100 Da) and dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, Lyso-CDs presenting excellent solubility in ethanol were obtained by drying the purified solution under a vacuum for 12 h.

Lyso-CDs were added to various pure solvents or water/1,4-dioxane mixtures at different ratios, fixing the concentration of Lyso-CDs at 40 μg/mL. Then, the fluorescence spectra were measured three times by spectrophotometry at λ
ex = 596 nm.

Imaging of cells: HeLa cells, MCF-7 cells, and HL-7702 cells were seeded into glass-bottomed dishes and incubated for 24 h. Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL) were introduced and cultured with cells for 5 min. After washing three times with PBS, the CDs-labeled cells were added to 1.5 mL of fresh DMEM and then employed for imaging. Finally, the fluorescence imaging was performed on the (CLSM) confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, LSM880).

In colocalization experiments, the cells were pre-stained with Lyso-Tracker Green (0.3 μM) for 30 min. Afterwards, the cells were cultured with Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL) for another 5 min. Subsequently, they were rinsed with PBS and imaged. Colocalization experiment with commercial Mito-Tracker green was conducted using a similar approach as mentioned above. (λex = 488 nm and 561 nm; green channel: λem = 508–558 nm, red channel: λem = 588–638 nm).

For the imaging of polarity, HeLa cells were incubated with Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL) for 5 min. Subsequently, DTT (80 mmol/L), DMSO (10 μL), and LPS (100 μg/mL) were utilized to image the changes in cell polarity. Confocal images were recorded on a Zeiss LSM 880.

Zebrafish embryos were grown in reproductive environments (28.5 °C) (Shanghai Fish Bio Co., Ltd., China) [34]. Zebrafish embryos were cultured with Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL, 5 min) at different growth stages (12, 24, 48, and 72 hpf). The stained embryos were instantly observed and recorded using a confocal fluorescence microscope. The imaging approach for zebrafish larvae was like that of embryonic zebrafish.

For polarity imaging of zebrafish, the 3-day-old zebrafish were cultured in fresh E3 culture medium at 28.5 °C. Zebrafish were cultured with Lyso-CDs (10 μg/mL) for 5 min and then soaked in a solution of DTT (80 mmol/L), DMSO (10 μL), or LPS (100 μg/mL) for another 60 min, respectively. During the imaging process, the zebrafish were anesthetized and shifted into new glass-bottomed dishes for imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, characterization, and morphology of the Lyso-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain ideal lysosome-targeted polarity-sensitive CDs featuring long-wavelength emission, the following issues should be taken into account. First, the CDs should have both electron-donating groups and electron-withdrawing groups in order to construct efficient ICT systems, thus endowing the CDs with excellent solvatochromism for polarity. Then, particular surface targeting species are necessary to allow CDs to specifically target lysosomes. Lastly, suitable aromatic structures would inherently reduce the energy gap through expanding larger π-conjugated domains, conducing to obtaining long-wavelength emissive CDs. CDs have been extensively presented as being able to preserve some certain characteristics of the precursors; consequently, CDs with desirable structures and features may be obtained through appropriately selecting the precursors [23]. In this work, we herein chose 3-nitrophenylboronic acid and 4-morpholinoaniline as raw materials by providing CDs with electron-donating groups (e.g., amino and morpholine groups) and electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., boronic and nitro groups) on the surface, thereby making sure their sensitive response to polarity and lysosome-targeting capability owing to the morpholine residue. In addition, it has been proven that morpholine could provide the lysosome-targeting ability [35]. Furthermore, Nile Blue, a class of phenoxazine dyes, the inherent conjugated electronic structure could be partly reserved during the dehydration and carbonization processes of the CDs synthesis [36], conducing to obtaining CDs with a large rigid sp2 conjugated structure. Together, we propose a method for generating lysosome-targeted polarity-sensitive CDs with red emission through one-pot solvothermal treatment of Nile Blue A, 3-nitrophenylboronic acid, and 4-morpholinoaniline in ethanol, as depicted in Scheme 1 .

The morphology of Lyso-CDs was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM results displayed that Lyso-CDs were sized of 3.75 nm (Fig. 1 a). Well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.20 nm could be observed in HRTEM images, attributable to the (100) plane of the graphitic facet (Fig. S1) [37]. Lyso-CDs were further scrutinized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. The characteristic peaks at 3435, 3084, 1612, 1349, and 1110 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, CO, B–C, and B–O–H (Fig. 1b) [38]. Besides, the peak at 1530 cm−1 of Lyso-CDs corresponded to the vibration of the nitro group in the nitrophenylboronic acid structure. The XPS spectra further confirmed the chemical compositions of the Lyso-CDs. The XPS full scan spectra revealed the consistency of C, O, N, and B elements with associated abundances of 45.79 %, 41.43 %, 10.45 %, and 2.31 %, respectively (Fig. 1c). The C 1s profile revealed that the Lyso-CDs contained three parts associated with C–B at 284.6 eV, C–N at 285.1 eV, and C–O at 286.1 eV (Fig. S2a). The N 1s XPS spectrum could be resolved into amino N (399.6 eV), =N–O (400.8 eV), and N–H (406.1 eV) bond peaks (Fig. 1d). Two peaks in the O 1s profile located at 532.7 eV and 533.3 eV, corresponding to C–O–C and = N–O (Fig. 1e). Also, the N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra show the existence of the nitro group. In addition, B 1 s spectra could be converted into two peaks at 191.4 eV and 192.2 eV, representing B–C, and B–O (Fig. S2b). To further elucidate the chemical surface groups of Lyso-CDs, the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S3) were performed, and several domain peaks ranging from 7.44 to 8.63 ppm could be ascribed to the ketene and phenyl protons. Additionally, the chemical shifts at 3.06 ppm and 3.75 ppm should be attributed to the hydrogen in the morpholine ring [39]. The chemical shifts at 124–147 and 29–40 ppm in the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S4) were suggestive of obvious aromatic-C and fatty-C, respectively. These results further confirmed that the as-prepared Lyso-CDs were considered to be composed of graphite-similar bones with defects triggered by N heterocycles. The XRD patterns of the Lyso-CDs had a strong peak at 27°, corresponding to the (002) crystalline facet of graphite (Fig. S5a) [40]. As shown in Fig. S5b, we observed two typical peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1376 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of Lyso-CDs assigned to the G and D bands of graphene, respectively. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) was measured to be 1.51, implying the presence of aromatic domains and a large portion of defects. Furthermore, the zeta potential of Lyso-CDs (Fig. S5c) was approximately −8.94 mV, confirming the existence of negative charges on Lyso-CDs.

Next, the optical characteristics of the Lyso-CDs were investigated. The main UV absorption peaks at 217 and 261 nm could be ascribed to the carbon core π-π* transition (Fig. 1f). In addition, the broad absorption band around 550 nm could excite Lyso-CDs, which corresponded to the typical n-π* transition of the heterocycle ring, or CO. The Lyso-CDs emitted red fluorescence with an maximum emission at 630 nm in ethanol, indicative of the promising deep-tissue permeability of Lyso-CDs. At the same time, the Lyso-CDs exhibited excitation-independent emissive states (Fig. S5d). Also, the fluorescence lifetime of the Lyso-CDs was 5.80 ns (Fig. S6a). The photostability of the Lyso-CDs was also discussed. After 1 h of continuous irradiation, there were no noticeable changes in the fluorescence intensity of the Lyso-CDs (Fig. S6b). The possible effect of ionic strength was also explored. The fluorescence intensity was almost constant at different concentrations of NaCl. (Fig. S6c). The observed stability suggests that Lyso-CDs could be efficiently used for biolabeling and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the Lyso-CDs to polarity
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent and absorption properties of Lyso-CDs were further verified in some common solvents with different polarities (Table S1). The Lyso-CDs exhibited solvent-engineered fluorescent properties on their maximum emission peak, gradually red-shifting from 582 nm to 650 nm with polarity changes in Fig. 2 a. Furthermore, the absorption peaks in various solvents slightly redshifted with increasing polarity, indicating excellent polarity-dependent behavior (Fig. S7a). The polarity sensitivity properties of Lyso-CDs were investigated using the 1,4-dioxane/H2O system (Table S2). The Lippert-Mataga polarity (Δf) was utilized to evaluate the polarity of solvents. An evident decrease in fluorescence intensity was found with the increase of polarity, accompanied by a red shift in the emission wavelength of Lyso-CDs to 650 nm (Fig. 2b and Fig. S7b). While the maximum absorption peaks in the H2O/1,4-dioxane mixed solvents exhibited a slight variation as the polarity changed (Fig. S7c). A favorable linear relationship was obtained between the fluorescence intensity and Δf within the range of Δf = 0.21 to 0.31 (R2 = 0.9934) (Fig. 2c). As shown in Table S3, the Lyso-CDs possess obvious advantages compared with previously reported polarity-sensitive CDs, which can be employed as a robust tool for imaging polarity changes [4,25–27,41].

The anti-interference behavior was used to assess the possibility of Lyso-CDs in living cells imaging. From Fig. 2d, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged in the presence of potential interfering substances, implying the high specificity of Lyso-CDs in biological systems. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of Lyso-CDs changed slightly at different pH values (Fig. 2e). Moreover, only a minor variation of fluorescence intensity of Lyso-CDs was found at different ratios of water/glycerol mixtures, which was negligible when compared with the influence of polarity (Fig. 2f). Therefore, Lyso-CDs could be considered suitable candidates for precisely quantifying polarity changes in biological applications.

The strong solvation effect inspired us to explore the possible sensing mechanism. Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) probe based on Lyso-CDs contains both electron donor and acceptor surface functional groups, and holds a large dipole moment. Also, due to the presence of functional groups such as –NH2, –OH and –NO2, Lyso-CDs could form hydrogen bond with the solvents of different polarities. The red-shifting and loss of fluorescence in polar solvents might be attributed to the interaction of dipole moment and hydrogen bond. Higher energy is needed to alter the dipole direction of adjacent solvent molecules as the polarity of the solvent increases. Consequently, excited electrons in a high polar solvent lose more energy than those in the low polar solvent, leading to a red shift of emission [42]. Moreover, the zeta potentials of the Lyso-CDs in solution with different polarities were measured. As shown in Fig. S8, the zeta potentials decreased from −0.50 mV to −8.94 mV with the introduction of water. This result offers strong evidence for the higher electron cloud density in solvents with high polarity, which is advantageous for emission with long wavelength [4]. Meanwhile, the increasing polarity of the solvent also could increase strength of hydrogen bond, further leading to an increase in internal conversion (IC) and vibrational relaxation (VR) efficiency. Due to the competition among IC, VR, and fluorescence, the enhancement of IC and VR also causes a decrease of the radiation transition, consequently resulting in a decrease of fluorescence intensity. In addition, a protonic polar solvent hinders the excited state of the Lyso-CDs from stabilizing, which facilitates the radiation-type transition and the reduction of fluorescence lifetime [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrarapid lysosome imaging of the Lyso-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In light of their fascinating properties, the potential applications of Lyso-CDs for bioimaging were also demonstrated. Before that, the biocompatibility of Lyso-CDs was estimated by the MTT experiments. The viability of HeLa cells was more than 95 % at the concentration of 10 μg/mL (Fig. S9). The result indicates low biological toxicity of the Lyso-CDs, suggesting that the Lyso-CDs have favorable biocompatibility. Cell fluorescence with distinct emissive intensity was observed within 30 s, suggesting the ultra-rapid capability to employ Lyso-CDs for labeling cells. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of Lyso-CDs remained stable within 30 min, demonstrating superior photostability of the Lyso-CDs (Fig. S10). Subsequent studies used a concentration of 10 μg/mL and a time of 5 min for the best imaging condition (Fig. S11).

To investigate the cellular staining specificity of Lyso-CDs, a colocalization experiment was performed. Fig. 3 a and S12 displayed that the red fluorescence channel of Lyso-CDs excellently overlapped with the commercial lysosomal green probe, and Pearson's correlation factors in HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells were measured to be 0.86 and 0.87, respectively. In contrast, poor overlap was found between the fluorescence signal of Lyso-CDs and Mito-Tracker Green, demonstrating that Lyso-CDs accumulate preferentially in lysosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the Lyso-CDs in detecting lysosomal polarity disorders
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by above excellent behavior of the Lyso-CDs, we explored the possibility of using Lyso-CDs to visualize polarity variations in lysosomes. Lysosomal storage disorders induced by high concentrations of DTT could cause lysosomal polarity increase, and DMSO could be absorbed rapidly and induce a decrease of lysosomal polarity [43]. Furthermore, it was also reported that LPS was conventionally used to induce variations of lysosomal polarity [44]. As expected, HeLa cells treated with DTT presented lower fluorescence than the control group. While fluorescence intensities of cells treated with DMSO or LPS were noticeably higher compare with the control group (Fig. 4 ). These results strongly suggest that Lyso-CDs could serve as a superb imaging agent for imaging the polarity changes of lysosomes.

Accumulating evidence suggests that the intracellular microenvironment between normal cells and cancer cells is different [6,11,13], prompting us to investigate whether Lyso-CDs could discriminate cancer cells from healthy cells via imaging lysosomal polarity. Cancer cells (HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells) stained with Lyso-CDs exhibited significantly stronger fluorescence; in contrast, normal cells (HL-7702 cells) showed weak fluorescence (Fig. 5 ). These results powerfully provided evidence for the feasibility of using Lyso-CDs to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring lysosomal polarity during autophagy progress
ce:section.ce:para: As a lysosomal degradation pathway, autophagy is critical for preserving intracellular homeostasis and disease regulation. The potential of Lyso-CDs for visualization of lysosomal polarity changes during autophagy was explored. To our knowledge, nutrient deficiency can interrupt the balance of energy metabolism, further promoting the occurrence of autophagy. The model of starvation was built by incubating cells with HBSS [16]. With the increase in starvation time, the cells displayed a gradually decreased fluorescent signal, indicating a gradual increase in lysosomal polarity under starvation (Fig. S13). Additionally, further studies have been carried out on the fluctuations during drug-induced autophagy. Rapamycin is frequently applied to induce autophagy [45]. As presented in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity in HeLa cells displayed a considerably continuous decrease over time. It could be attributed to the fact that rapamycin induces more severe autophagy than starvation. Furthermore, the cells stained with Lyso-CDs were incubated with autophagy inhibitors (HBSS with 3-MA or rapamycin with chloroquine). However, the fluorescence in cells presented a constantly bright emission signal, demonstrating that autophagy was inhibited (Fig. S14). Thus, Lyso-CDs can be used to dynamically visualize the polarity changes in the lysosome during autophagy via fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of polarity in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the cell imaging results shown above, we then evaluated the behavior of Lyso-CDs for imaging in vivo. Zebrafish are selected as the imaging objects due to their rapid growth and high optical transparency [46]. According to earlier studies, the polarity in zebrafish may continuously fluctuate at different development stages [5,15]. Therefore, embryo and adult zebrafish stained with Lyso-CDs were performed for imaging. In the presence of Lyso-CDs, a clear red fluorescence signal could be found in both embryos and larvae. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity in zebrafish was remarkably stronger in the zebrafish embryonic state than in larvae (Fig. 7 ), which is consistent with the previous work. This difference indicates that polarity presents a gradual increase during the zebrafish growth process.

Furthermore, we examined the fluctuations of polarity in the zebrafish model of drug stimulation. In brief, DTT, DMSO, and LPS were also applied to change the polarity of zebrafish. Compared with zebrafish treated only with Lyso-CDs, zebrafish treated with LPS or DMSO exhibited remarkably enhanced fluorescence (Fig. 8 ). However, the treatment of zebrafish with DTT presented a significant decrease in the fluorescence of the red channel. These results were in agreement with the observations of drug-stimulated cellular imaging, indicating that Lyso-CDs can monitor polarity changes in vivo.

In conclusion, we have designed and successfully developed a novel red-emissive CDs for monitoring lysosomal polarity variations. By a one-pot solvothermal treatment of Nile Blue A, 3-nitrophenylboronic acid, and 4-morpholinoaniline, Lyso-CDs covered with electron-donating groups and electron-withdrawing groups were simply obtained. Specifically, Lyso-CDs showed a high sensitivity response to polarity due to the formation of effective ICT systems. All these features, such as red emission, rapid staining, and excellent lysosome specificity, enable Lyso-CDs to monitor the variations of lysosomal polarity efficiently in both cells and zebrafish. Using Lyso-CDs, the visualization of cancerous cells, autophagy progress, and zebrafish development stage have been successfully achieved by imaging lysosomal polarity. We expect that our probe Lyso-CDs can serve as a powerful tool for promoting lysosomal polarity-related fundamental and clinical research. Our work can also shed new light on the design strategy of future fluorescent carbon dots in terms of structure and functionality for precisely imaging of other organelles and tracking multiple dynamic behaviors using advanced imaging techniques.

Jianhua Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Data curation. Jinling Xing: Investigation. Hongmei Xu: Supervision. Zhenhua Yang: Supervision. Wenjing Lu: Supervision. Chuan Dong: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.22274090), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2022Y024). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the NMR and Raman analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2024.101940 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2024.101940.
